meta.title: .rlockie Syntax Reference

file.name: ReadlockCopywritingGuide.md
file.role: content authority (tone, voice, phase structure, lengths, all writing rules)

meta.authority_note: This file defines how .rlockie files are structured: what blocks exist, what fields each block needs, and how to format them. It is NOT the content authority. For tone, voice, register, phase structure, swipe length, consequence/hint rules, reflect content, color highlight meanings, and every other "what do I actually write" question, the authority is ReadlockCopywritingGuide.md. If anything here contradicts the copywriting guide, the copywriting guide wins.

section.name: Metadata writing rules
section.description: The copywriting guide covers @text, @question, and other content blocks. Metadata fields (title, description, author, segment titles) are not covered by that guide, but they must follow the same register.
section.principle: Write metadata the way a person would describe a book to a friend, not the way a marketing team would pitch it to investors.
section.example: Explore the fascinating world of cognitive biases and unlock powerful psychological principles that will transform your design thinking.
How cognitive biases shape the way people interact with designed objects.
A comprehensive deep dive into the art and science of color theory.
Why colors make people feel, and how designers have used that for centuries.

rules.rule: Every rule in ReadlockCopywritingGuide.md Sections 2 and 4 applies to metadata as well (banned verbs, banned openers, banned closers, banned inflators, casual slang, em dashes, triple-anything lists).
Descriptions state what the course is about. They do not sell it.
Segment titles are short, plain nouns or noun phrases ("Foundations," "Perception and Decisions"). Not taglines.
If a sentence could appear in any course about any topic and still make sense, it is too generic. Rewrite it to be specific to this course.

section.name: Document structure
section.description: A .rlockie file has three levels of hierarchy:

level.note: exactly once, first line of the file
one or more, groups packages into chapters
one or more per segment, separates lessons

rules.rule: Every line that starts with @ is a DECLARATION. It must appear on its own line with nothing else on that line.
Everything that follows a declaration (until the next @declaration) is that block's BODY.

section.name: @course
section.description: Must be the very first line. Followed by course metadata as key: value pairs.
section.template:
@course
id: [course identifier] language: [two-letter uppercase language code, e.g. EN] title: [course display title] author: [author display name] description: [one-line description] color: [one of the 12 allowed swatches, e.g. 8461BD — NO leading #] genres: [3 to 5 comma-separated keys from the closed list, e.g. design, psychology, productivity] relevant_for: [comma-separated keys from the closed list: designers, product-managers, founders, engineers, writers, marketers, leaders, investors, students, anyone]

rules.rule: All eight fields are required.
One blank line between @course and the first field.
language is a two-letter uppercase code (e.g. EN, PL, DE). Defaults to EN when omitted.
description must be a single line.

color must be exactly one of these 12 hex values, WITHOUT a leading "#": 9E7071, D0CBD6, C78871, FAF0A2, 8461BD, 78A3ED, 72ACAD, C85159, 6D7487, CAC7EE, 6A99D4, FFC1E2. The "#" is never written in the .rlockie file (e.g. "color: 8461BD", not "color: #8461BD"). Pick the swatch closest to the original book cover when available; otherwise pick the swatch that best fits the topic's mood. Never invent a new hex value.

genres must contain 3 to 5 comma-separated tags drawn from this closed list ONLY: design, psychology, business, technology, self-help, science, philosophy, history, economics, leadership, creativity, productivity, communication, user-experience, product-development. Never invent a new genre. Pick the closest tag from the list instead of coining a new one.
relevant_for values must come from the closed list: designers, product-managers, founders, engineers, writers, marketers, leaders, investors, students, anyone.

type.name: book
type.description: for courses based on a book

type.name: topic
type.description: for courses about a standalone topic

type.name: article
type.description: for courses based on an article

id_format.rule: The 4-char hash is randomly generated from lowercase a-z.
The kebab-case title is a shortened version of the course title, not the full title.

section.name: @segment
section.description: Defines a chapter/section. Body is exactly two lines.

rules.rule: A course must have at least one segment.
The symbol is a short abbreviation (e.g. "BD" for "Basic Design").
Every segment must be followed by at least one @package.

section.name: @package
section.description: Separates packages (lessons) within a segment. The lines immediately after @package (before the first content block) are package metadata.

rules.rule: The first non-empty line after @package is the package display title.
If the word "Free" appears on its own line, the package is marked as free.
Both are optional. If no title is provided, a default "Package N" is used.
Every package must contain at least one content block.
The title and Free flag must come before any @text, @question, or other content declarations.

block.name: @text
block.description: A reading slide. Each line becomes a separate text segment displayed one at a time.
block.template: @text First line shown to the learner. Second line shown after the first. A third line with a <c:g>highlighted topic term</c:g>.

line_rules.rule: Each line is one on-screen segment.
One sentence per line is the default, up to two sentences allowed when they read as a single beat, three is never allowed.
Every line, whether one or two sentences, fits in about 16 words total.

color.meaning: Marks the TOPIC of the current package (the concept, study, book, or effect the package is about).
Marks a WRONG thing stated inside the text (a misconception, a deliberately wrong statement the narrator is about to correct). Never on the topic, never on anything correct.

image_link.template: @text The water cycle is one of the most elegant systems on Earth. image-link: "Water cycle diagram" Look at the graphic.
image_link.description: The description is used to search Wikimedia Commons. Write it like a librarian tagging a photograph, not like a designer describing a mockup.

rules.rule: Use broad, categorical terms that real photographs and diagrams are tagged with.
Use the actual subject name, not a creative interpretation of it.
Prefer nouns over adjectives. "Human eye anatomy diagram" not "detailed beautiful eye illustration."
Add "diagram" or "photograph" when the type of image matters.
Ask yourself: "Would a Wikipedia article plausibly use this image?" If yes, good.

examples.example: Crowd of people in a city street
A futuristic holographic brain with neural pathways glowing in blue
Minimal flat illustration of cognitive load theory
Beautiful sunset over the ocean representing peace
An elegant visualization of the anchoring effect

image_link.rule: When an image is included, refer to it in the surrounding text: "Look at the graphic" or "The diagram shows how..." Never leave an image floating without context.

rules.rule: At least one line of text.

Color markup is rare: maximum 1 to 2 highlights per WHOLE PACKAGE across both colours combined, not per text block. Most @text blocks will contain zero highlights. The one that names the topic gets the green one.
At most 1 color highlight per @text swipe. Do not put two highlights in the same swipe.

block.name: @question
block.template:
@question [Question text - one line]
answer: [First answer option] consequence: [Feedback shown when this answer is picked]
answer: [Second answer option] consequence: [Feedback shown when this answer is picked]
answer: [Third answer option] consequence: [Feedback shown when this answer is picked]
correct_answers: [comma-separated 1-based indices, e.g. 1 or 1,3] explanation: [Explanation shown after answering] hint: [Hint shown before answering]

rules.rule: Question text must be exactly one line.
Minimum 2 answers, maximum 3.
Each answer must be immediately followed by its consequence.
Blank line between each answer+consequence pair.
correct_answers uses 1-based indexing (1 = first answer).
Both hint and explanation are required.
Each consequence is 1 to 3 sentences, each sentence at most about 14 words (can go to 17-18).
Each hint is exactly one sentence, at most 14 words (can go to 17-18).

block.name: @true_false
block.description: A statement the learner must judge as true or false. The two answer options are always literally "True" and "False" — never descriptive text.

rules.rule: Statement must be exactly one line.
Exactly one true: and one false: line, in that order.
The two answer options are always literally "True" and "False". Never descriptive text, never paraphrased, never restating the statement.
The text after true: and false: is the consequence shown after the reader picks that option, not the option label.
correct_answers is 1 if the statement is true, 2 if false.
hint is required, and follows the same rules as @question hints (one sentence, ~14 words).
Consequences follow the same rules as @question consequences (1 to 3 sentences).
The statement should feel semi-obvious after a moment of thought, not a trap and not dumb.

block.name: @estimate
block.description: A percentage estimation question. The learner drags a slider to guess.
block.template: @estimate [Question asking for a percentage] answer: [correct number, 0-100] explanation: [Explanation] hint: [Hint text]

rules.rule: Question must be exactly one line.
answer must be an integer between 0 and 100.
hint is required.

block.name: @quote

rules.rule: First line is the quote (no quotation marks, the UI adds them).
Second line is the author attribution. Can be left empty.

block.name: @pause
block.description: A breather slide with a nice animation and a single sentence. Gives the reader a beat of rest between denser swipes.

rules.rule: Exactly one line, exactly one sentence. Never two.
The sentence is a small interesting fact, observation, or reframing related to the topic of the package. Not trivia, not a quiz, not a recap, not a motivational line.
Never starts with "Did you know...", "Fun fact:", or "Pro tip:".
Zero or one @pause per package. Default spot is after Phase 3 (questions), before Phase 4 (topic reveal). Must not name or hint at the topic.

block.name: @reflect
block.description: A reflection prompt. Each line is one thinking point.
block.template: @reflect [First thinking point, including one concrete example] [Second thinking point, including one concrete example] [Third thinking point, including one concrete example]

rules.rule: 2 to 3 thinking points, one per line.
Each point is about 14 to 16 words.
Each point contains one concrete example (a situation, an object, a moment, or a type of person). A reflect prompt without an anchor fails.
No platitudes, no fridge-magnet wisdom.

section.name: Formatting rules
section.rule: Blank lines are meaningful. They separate logical sections within a block.
No trailing whitespace on any line.
No blank lines inside the body of @quote, @pause, or @estimate.
One blank line between the last content block and the next @package or @segment.
Properties (answer:, consequence:, correct_answers:, explanation:, hint:, true:, false:) are NOT declarations. They use "key: value" syntax with a colon and space.

section.name: Comments
section.description: The .rlockie format supports full-line comments. Comments are a writer-only feature: the parser strips them before any other parsing happens, so they never reach the reader and never affect block boundaries, properties, or text segments.
section.syntax: Any line whose first non-whitespace character is "#" is a comment.
section.template:
# This is a comment. It will be stripped before parsing. # Use it for notes, TODOs, draft markers, or to temporarily disable a swipe.
@text First line of text.

rules.rule: A comment is recognised by its first non-whitespace character being "#". Leading whitespace before the "#" is allowed.
Comments are FULL-LINE only. Inline comments (text followed by "#" on the same line) are NOT supported.
Comments can sit anywhere in the file: between blocks, inside blocks, between properties, even between an answer: line and its consequence: line. The parser strips them before block splitting.
To temporarily disable a swipe, prefix every line of the swipe (including the @declaration line) with "#".
Comments do NOT count toward any length rule, swipe count, or character budget. They are invisible to the parser and to ReadlockFinalTest.xml.

Comments are highlighted in the same muted grey (#9ca3af, italic) that property names (answer:, consequence:, hint:, explanation:) use, so comments sit quietly in the same visual register as format scaffolding rather than content.

example_use_cases.case: Marking a draft section: # DRAFT, needs a stronger anchor in Phase 2
Leaving a note for another writer: # Verify the 1990s ATM study citation before publishing
Temporarily disabling a swipe by prefixing every line with "#".
Explaining a non-obvious choice: # Using misconception-first intro here because the reader almost certainly believes the wrong picture

mandatory_package_structure_comment.description: Every @package MUST start with TWO comment lines. The first lists the swipe-type sequence; the second summarises the package's creative choices (Phase 2 variant, intro type, concept taught). Together they give a writer-facing index that makes both the shape AND the intent of the package visible at a glance before reading any content. Both comments are required for every package, in every course.
mandatory_package_structure_comment.placement: Both comment lines go IMMEDIATELY after the package title line (and after the "Free" line if the package is free), and BEFORE the first content block. Line 1 (swipe sequence) goes first, then line 2 (package summary) directly underneath.

line.label: Swipe sequence
line.syntax: A single line that begins with "#" and lists the swipe types (with the "@" prefix) separated by commas, in the order they appear inside the package. The list includes EVERY swipe: intro, stories, question, optional pause, reveal, optional estimate, true/false, optional reflect, outro.

rules.rule: The list lists swipe types in the same order they appear in the package body.
Every swipe in the package appears in the list. The list length equals the swipe count.

If the package uses an intro variant (skip-intro, quote intro, true/false intro, misconception-first), the swipe list reflects it. A quote intro starts with "@quote". A skip-intro package starts with "@text" (the first story).

line.label: Package summary
line.syntax: A single line that begins with "#" and contains three comma-separated fields in this exact order: (1) Phase 2 variant letter with content descriptor in parentheses, (2) intro type, (3) "teaching: " followed by the concept name.

rules.rule: Field 1 format: "Variant X (descriptor)". X is A through G. The descriptor names what Phase 2 actually contains, not always "stories". Use the descriptor that matches the variant: Variant A = "N scenes", Variant B = "single narrative" or "N beats", Variant C = "N stories", Variant D = "N stories", Variant E = "N steps" or "mechanism walkthrough", Variant F = "historical arc", Variant G = "thought experiment". A package that does not literally contain stories must not say "stories".
Field 2 values: "default intro" | "quote intro" | "true/false intro" | "misconception intro" | "skip intro". Exactly one of these.
Field 3 format: "teaching: [concept name]" where the concept is the term that carries the green <c:g> highlight in Phase 4. Lowercase, no article, no quotes.
Fields are separated by a comma and a single space. No em dashes, no semicolons.

mandatory_package_structure_comment.template:
@package The Pretty Machine That Worked Better Free # @text, @text, @text, @question, @text, @true_false, @text # Variant C (2 stories), default intro, teaching: the aesthetic-usability effect
@text Two coffee makers sit on the same kitchen counter. ...

rules.rule: Both comment lines are required, not optional. Every package has both.
The two comments are stripped by the parser before any other parsing, so they never reach the reader.
A package missing either comment line is an error.

example.label: Default spine, Variant C
example.code: # @text, @text, @text, @text, @question, @text, @true_false, @reflect, @text # Variant C (3 stories), default intro, teaching: the habit loop
example.description: Intro, three stories, question, reveal, true/false, reflect, outro. Phase 2 variant C (one extended case built across three swipes).

example.label: Quote intro, Variant A
example.code: # @quote, @text, @text, @question, @text, @true_false, @text # Variant A (2 stories), quote intro, teaching: the anchoring effect
example.description: Quote intro, two reader-immersion stories, question, reveal, true/false, outro. Phase 6 reflect skipped.

example.label: True/false intro, Variant E, Phase 5 empty
example.code: # @true_false, @text, @text, @question, @text, @text # Variant E (2 stories), true/false intro, teaching: the power law
example.description: True/false intro replaces Phase 5. Two experiment/findings stories, question, reveal, outro.

example.label: Skip-intro, Variant D
example.code: # @text, @text, @text, @question, @pause, @text, @true_false, @reflect, @text # Variant D (3 stories), skip intro, teaching: definite optimism
example.description: Phase 1 intro skipped. Package opens directly at Phase 2 with three scenes showing the same pattern. Pause before the reveal.

example.label: Misconception-first, Variant B
example.code: # @text, @text, @text, @question, @text, @true_false, @text # Variant B (2 beats), misconception intro, teaching: the visceral level
example.description: Misconception-first intro looks like a normal @text intro in line 1. The misconception is signalled inside the swipe by the <c:r> markup, and line 2 names it. Variant B is a single narrative, so the descriptor counts beats, not stories.

example.label: Quote intro, Variant G
example.code: # @quote, @text, @text, @question, @text, @true_false, @text # Variant G (thought experiment), quote intro, teaching: progressive disclosure
example.description: Variant G builds the case from a hypothetical instead of stories. The descriptor is "thought experiment", never "N stories", because there are no characters or real scenes to count.

example.label: Default intro, Variant F
example.code: # @text, @text, @text, @text, @question, @text, @true_false, @text # Variant F (historical arc), default intro, teaching: the flywheel
example.description: Variant F is one long story told across eras. The descriptor is "historical arc", not "3 stories", because it is one continuous arc split across swipes.
